behaviors.description: All direct and transitive dependencies are extracted from the manifest and included in the vulnerability check
behaviors.justification: Extracting transitive dependencies requires testing against real manifest file formats and dependency trees. A unit test with only direct dependencies in mock data would pass even if transitive extraction is broken. Real manifest files (package.json, requirements.txt, lock files) are needed to verify the complete dependency tree is discovered, including nested transitive dependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Load a real project manifest with known direct and transitive dependencies, call the extraction function, and verify all expected dependencies are included. Compare against a golden list of dependencies for that project. Test with multiple manifest formats (npm, pip, composer, etc.).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration tests using file I/O might miss failures if the implementation relies on subprocess calls to tools like 'npm ls' or 'pip freeze' to enumerate dependencies. System-level tests invoking the actual enumeration tools would be more robust.

behaviors.description: The vulnerability database query returns results that match the exact version ranges in the manifest, not just the package name
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on real API interaction. Mocking the vulnerability database API would hide failures where the actual API rejects the query format or returns unexpected results. You must verify that version range queries (e.g., '>=1.0.0,<2.0.0') are formatted correctly for the real API and that the API actually matches versions against the manifest range.
behaviors.test_description: Query a real vulnerability database API (or a test instance with predictable data) with version ranges extracted from actual manifests. Verify the API returns CVEs that match the specified version range. Test boundary cases like exact version pins, range operators (>, >=, <, <=, ~, ^), and multi-part version numbers.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real API calls will reveal whether version range formatting is correct and whether the API's version matching logic aligns with the manifest format.

behaviors.description: The severity sorting places Critical above High above Medium above Low, with CVEs of the same severity sorted by CVSS score
behaviors.justification: This is a pure sorting algorithm concern. The failure mode (reversed severity order, wrong CVSS comparison) can be caught with unit tests using mock CVE data. You can construct test data with known severity levels and CVSS scores and verify the sort order.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock CVE objects with various severity levels (Critical, High, Medium, Low) and CVSS scores. Call the sort function and verify: (1) Severity order is Critical > High > Medium > Low, (2) Within each severity group, CVEs are sorted by CVSS score (descending), (3) Edge cases like same CVSS scores and missing fields are handled correctly.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit tests with mock data might miss failures if the real vulnerability database API returns severity in a different format or uses unexpected field names. Integration/System tests with actual API responses would catch format-related failures.

behaviors.description: The manifest modification changes only the affected dependency's version, leaving all other entries unchanged
behaviors.justification: File modification logic requires real manifest files and I/O to verify. A unit test with string/dict mocking would hide failures where the file format is corrupted (e.g., JSON becomes invalid, YAML indentation breaks, comments are lost). You need to write actual files and verify they remain valid after modification.
behaviors.test_description: Create test manifest files (package.json, requirements.txt, etc.) with multiple dependencies. Call the modification function to bump a single dependency version. Read back the modified file and verify: (1) Only the target dependency's version changed, (2) All other dependencies are unchanged, (3) File format is valid and preserves comments/whitespace where applicable, (4) File structure (JSON/YAML syntax) is intact.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration tests with real manifest files will catch syntax errors and format preservation issues that string-based mocks would miss.

behaviors.description: The auto-generated pull request is created on the real repository with the correct single-dependency diff
behaviors.justification: Creating a real pull request with the correct diff requires actual GitHub API calls and a real repository. Mocking the GitHub API would hide failures where the API rejects the diff format, branch creation fails, or the diff is computed incorrectly. The diff must be verified against real repository state.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test repository (use GitHub's API or a local test repo). Call the PR creation logic with a manifest change. Verify: (1) A new branch is created with the correct name, (2) The PR is created with the correct diff (only the affected dependency's version changed), (3) The PR metadata (title, description, branch) is correct, (4) The diff can be applied cleanly, (5) No extra files or changes are included.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System tests with real repository API calls will verify PR creation, branch handling, and diff correctness against actual GitHub behavior.

behaviors.description: A major version bump is correctly identified as a breaking change based on semver comparison
behaviors.justification: Semver comparison is deterministic logic. Failures (reversed comparison, incorrect parsing) can be caught with unit tests using version string pairs. No real infrastructure or I/O is needed to verify semver logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create unit tests with pairs of version strings covering: (1) Major version changes (1.0.0 → 2.0.0 = breaking), (2) Minor/patch changes (1.0.0 → 1.1.0 = not breaking), (3) Prerelease versions (1.0.0-alpha → 1.0.0 = not breaking), (4) Version formats with 'v' prefix (v1.0.0 vs 1.0.0), (5) Complex ranges like ^1.0.0 and ~1.0.0. Verify correct detection of major changes.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Semver comparison is pure logic; unit tests with version pairs will reliably catch comparison errors without requiring external dependencies.

behaviors.description: When a breaking change is detected, PR creation is skipped and a review flag is surfaced in the scanner output
behaviors.justification: The core logic—conditional branching (if breaking change, skip PR and set flag)—can be tested with unit tests using mock breaking change data. The question is whether the code executes the right path when a major version is detected.
behaviors.test_description: Mock a breaking change scenario (major version bump detected) and verify: (1) The PR creation function is not called, (2) A review flag is set in the output, (3) The output message clearly indicates why PR was skipped (breaking change detected), (4) Test with non-breaking changes to verify PR is created normally.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit tests with mock data might miss failures in the full system flow (e.g., real API returning unexpected breaking change data, flag not propagating through all output formats). System-level end-to-end tests with real manifests and API calls would be more robust.
